titleStmt.title: Two-dimensional Penta-NiPS Sheets: Two Stable Polymorphs

affiliation.orgName: College of Materials Innovation and Technology
King Mongkut's Institute of Technology Ladkrabang
Center of Excellence in Advanced Functional Materials
College of Materials Innovation and Technology
King Mongkut's Institute of Technology Ladkrabang
Center of Excellence in Advanced Functional Materials

analytic.title: Two-dimensional Penta-NiPS Sheets: Two Stable Polymorphs

application.desc: GROBID - A machine learning software for extracting information from scholarly documents

div.p: The discovery of new and stable two-dimensional (2D) materials with exotic properties is essential for technological advancement. Inspired by the recently reported penta-PdPSe, we proposed penta-NiPS as a new member of the penta-2D materials based on first-principles calculations. The penta-NiPS monolayer is stable in two polymorphs including the α phase with identical structure as penta-PdPSe, and the newly proposed β phase with rotated sublayers. Comprehensive analyses indicated that both phases are thermodynamically, dynamically, mechanically, and thermally stable. The penta-NiPS is a soft material with 2D Young's modulus of E a = 208 Nm -1 and E b = 178 Nm -1 for the α phase and E a = 184 Nm -1 and E b = 140 Nm -1 for the β phase. Interestingly, the α-penta-NiPS showed nearly zero Poisson's ratios along the in-plane direction, where its dimensions would be maintained when being extended.
For electronic application, we demonstrated that penta-NiPS is the wide band gap semiconductors with an indirect band gap of 2.35 eV for α phase, and 2.20 eV for β phase.

div.head: Introduction
div.p: Since the discovery of hexacyclic graphene, two-dimensional (2D) materials have been rapidly advanced in terms of production and materials design. An increasing demand on their discovery has been driving the theoretical explorations of stable 2D materials beyond the conventional honeycomb-like structure, including the understanding of composition-structureproperties relationships. Recently, Zhang et al. 1 theoretically proposed a new pentagonal structure penta-graphene with carbon atoms uniquely resembling Cairo pentagonal tiling.

Although not yet experimentally synthesized, the properties of the penta-graphene have been widely explored through first-principles calculations. The wide band gap of 3.25 eV and high strength could lead to potential applications in nanoelectronics and nanomechanical devices.

Other prominent examples are SiC 2 , CN 2 , BN 2 , B 2 C, PdS 2 , AlN 2 , PtN 2 , PdN 2 , and NiS 2 . [2][3][4][5][6][7][8][9] In all cases, it is essential that one thoroughly explores various aspects of materials' stability including dynamic, mechanical, and thermal ones.

The investigation of ternary compounds provides a higher degree of freedom in tuning the properties of penta-2D materials. 10 The prominent example is penta-BCN 10 which is thermodynamically, mechanically, and thermally stable according to density functional theory (DFT), in addition to possessing a high piezoelectric response. Later on, the penta-PdPSe with intrinsic in-plane anisotropy has been experimentally synthesized. 11 This material is made up from two PdPSe sublayers which are uncommon in nature, leading to unusual electronic and optical properties. 12,13 Nevertheless, penta-PdPSe is the closely related member of the binary penta-PdSe 2 which can be readily synthesized via mechanical exfoliation from bulk crystals. 14 It has been experimentally demonstrated that the penta-PdSe 2 based field-effect transistors (FETs) exhibited band gap tunability and high electron mobility. 14 Accordingly, chemical intuition with appropriate atom substitution is a simple yet potential method for theoretically discovering novel materials.

Herein, we used first-principles calculations to search for a novel member in the ternary pentagonal sheet family. Based on the reported atomic configuration of penta-PdPSe, Pd was replaced with Ni, and Se was replaced with S in this work. We found that the proposed composition penta-NiPS exists in two polymorphs which are thermodynamically, dynamically, mechanically, and thermally stable. The α phase has the structure similar to penta-PdPSe.

Meanwhile, another stable structure, the β phase, can be constructed by rotating the sublayers from the α phase. Notably, the α phase exhibited zero Poisson's ratio (ZPR) in the principle x and y directions (i.e., along the sheet), a unique ability to maintain its shape when being compressed or extended. This is rather unusual because most solids exhibit a positive Poisson's ratio (PPR) in the range of 0.2-0.3, 15 which describes the level of transversally deformation under an axial strain. The electronic and mechanical properties of two phase have also been compared.

div.head: Computational Method
div.p: Our calculations are based on density functional theory (DFT) and the plane-wave basis projector augmented wave (PAW) 16 method, as defined in the Vienna Ab-initio Simulation Package (VASP). 17,18 The kinetic energy cutoff of 500 eV is used for the plane-wave expansion. Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof (PBE) 19 functional is imposed for geometry optimization. In order to accurately describe layered material, the long-range van der Waals (vdW) interactions were taken into account using the DFT-D2 functional proposed by Grimme. 20 The structures are optimized until total Hellmann-Feynman forces are decreased to 0.02 eV/ Åand the energy convergence is set to 10 -6 eV. The centered k-point grid within 7 × 7 × 1 is employed for first Brillouin zone integration within the Monkhorst-Pack 21 scheme. A vacuum layer of 16 Å is included along the direction perpendicular to a crystal layer to avoid the interaction between the layer and its neighboring image. A PHONOPY package 22 over 3×3×1 supercell with 500 eV of energy cutoff and a single k-point at Γ is used to calculate phonon spectra to verify the dynamic stability. Ab initio molecular dynamics (AIMD) simulations is employed to confirm thermodynamic stability. To perform electronic band structure, since DFT within the standard approximation severely underestimates band gaps, we use the Heyd-Scuseria-Ernzerhof (HSE06) 23,24 hybrid functional to correct not only the band gap but also the band width. The electronic properties of α-and β-NiPS phases are first elucidated by the calculated band structure as shown in Fig. 2. The dash and solid lines are energy bands calculated from PBE and HSE06 functional, respectively. By using the PBE functional, 19 the electronic band structures show indirect band gaps of 0.88 and 0.68 eV for α and β phase, respectively. As it is well-known that the standard DFT underestimates band gap, the HSE06 functional 23,24 was also employed. It is found that a larger band gap of 2.35 eV was obtained for the α To evaluate the crystal binding stability, the cohesive energy per atom was calculated,

div.head: Results and discussion
div.p: given by the following equation:
div.formula: E coh = E tot (NiPS) -n i E i n i(1)
div.p: where E tot (NiPS) is the total energy of the penta-NiPS, n i is the number of Ni, P and S atoms in the unit cell and E i represents the energies of an isolated single Ni, P and S atom. The calculated cohesive energy of the penta-NiPS are -5.44 and -5.42 eV/atom for the α and β phase, respectively. The negative values indicate that the penta-NiPS are more energetically favorable than the isolated atoms. In addition, the α phase is just slighlty more energetically favorable than the β phase. Generally, different polymorphs (e.g., anatase vs rutile for TiO 2 ) could be synthesized by a fine tuning of synthetic conditions such as temperature, pressure, surrounding gas, among others. 26 The selective synthesis of either α and β penta-NiPS remains to be accomplished.

We verify the dynamic stability of α and β penta-NiPS by calculating their phonon dispersion. The dynamic stability manifests itself as the positive frequencies in the phonon spectra throughout the entire Brillouin zone. On the other hand, if the spectra display imaginary frequency modes (negative values), the structures reveal non-restorative force against a displacement of atoms, indicating instability. in) is a well-known computational error which can be effectively eliminated using the LDA functional, for example. These negative frequencies of phonon dispersion are also observed in penta-PdSe 2 . 14 Mechanical stability is an important property for strain engineering. Such stability can be verified from the linear elastic constants, C 11 , C 22 , C 12 and C 66 , which are elements in the stiffness tensor. Here, the elastic constants are obtained by finite difference method.

Our calculated elastic constants of both polymorphs are listed in table 2. Typically, the structures are considered mechanically stable when the linear elastic constants satisfy the conditions C 11 C 22 -C 2 12 > 0 and C 66 > 0. Notably, these conditions are obeyed for both phases, confirming the mechanical stability of penta-NiPS.

Next, we evaluated the 2D Young's modulus on the [100] and [010] directions (i.e., along the plane), which is obtained by To further explore the dependence of mechanical properties on the crystal orientation of penta-NiPS, we computed the Young's modulus (E) and Poisson's ratio (ν) along an arbitrary direction θ (θ is the angle relative to the x direction) using the following formulas: For practical application of semiconductors, it is beneficial that one can modify the electronic properties by a simple method such as strain engineering. This can be effectively applied to 2D materials via lattice mismatch on the substrate, thermal expansion, or mechanical loading. Because 2D materials tend to fold up under lateral compression, we focused on the tensile strain only, specifically the biaxial strain effect. The degree of strain is defined as ε = ∆l/l 0 × 100, where ∆l is the difference in a strained length, l, and an original, unstrained length, l 0 . Fig. 5 is the plot showing the strain energies, E S , and their derivative (dE S (ε)/dε) with respect to the applied strains from 0 -20%. For simplicity, the energies of the strain-free structures are set to zero so that the strain energies, E S is the energy differences between the total energies of the structure with and without strain. When the biaxial strain is continuously applied, a crystal expands until reaching the mechanical failure at ε = ε C where the maximum derivative of strain is observed. The mechanical failure of the α-NiPS and β-NiPS occurs at ε α C = 16.90% and 12.66%, respectively. These values can be compared with 24.3% for penta-BCN, and 17.5% for hydrogenated penta-BCN (H-BCN). 28,29 Normally, 2D materials can sustain much larger strains than their bulk structure. Also, 2D materials with buckling structure can withstand a higher strain than the non-buckling structure. Here, the resilience of the α phase could be linked to the bucking height T 1 compared to that of the β phase.
It is known that the band gaps of 2D materials can be potentially modulated by strain.

Thus, we also investigated how the band gaps of α-NiPS and β-NiPS change with the additional strain, starting from their equilibrium (i.e., zero strain) up to just prior to the critical point of strain. In Fig. S1( The thermal stability of penta-NiPS was examined via ab initio molecular dynamic simulations. Supercells with a duplication of a 4 × 4 × 1 of the unit cell is used. The molecular dynamics simulations were performed for 15 ps with a time step of 1 fs under the temperature from 300 to 800 K. Fig. 6 shows the fluctuation of the potential energy of penta-NiPS throughout the simulation time at 800 K. For both phases, the respective structure reaches the steady state and equilibrates around a constant energy after 1,000 fs. These results clearly indicate the thermal stability of penta-NiPS at least up to 800 K.

It is true that both polymorphs of NiPS has not been experimentally realized. Still, the related composition MPS 3 (M = Mn, Fe, Co, Ni and Cd) is a classic example of layered compounds crystallizing in CdCl 2 -type structure. 30 Although the two structures (α-and β-penta-NiPS vs CdCl 2 ) are clearly different, NiPS might be experimentally synthesized. Consider, for example, PdSe 2 which can be successfully synthesized by a self-flux method. 14 Moreover, very recently, penta-PdPS (the same structure with α-NiPS) can be successfully synthesized by exfoliated from bulk crystals via chemical vapor transport (CVT) method. 31 It is premature to explicitly suggest the synthetic procedure at present, but we hope it could be achievable.

div.head: Conclusions
div.p: By using DFT calculation, we theoretically discovered penta-NiPS as the new member in the penta-2D family. The penta-NiPS is stable in two polymorphs. The α-NiPS has the same structure as the previously reported penta-PdPSe, while the β-NiPS was obtained by rotating the two sublayers from the α-NiPS. Both phases are dynamically, mechanically, and thermally stable, as comprehensively verified from phonon dispersion, elastic constants, and molecular dynamic simulation, respectively. We found that the penta-NiPS is a semicon-

figure.head: Figure 1 :
figure.figDesc: Figure 1: 3x3 supercell of pentagonal (a) α-NiPS and (b) β-NiPS geometrical structures.

phase, vs 2.20 eV for the β phase, with the indirect band gap character being consistent. As seen in Fig.2within HSE06, the valence band maximum (VBM) of α phase is located along the M -Y path and the conduction band minimum (CBM) at the M point. In the β phase, the VBM is located along the Γ -Y path and the CBM is at the Γ point.

figure.head: Figure 2 :
figure.figDesc: Figure 2: Calculated band structure and partial density of state (PDOS) for (a) α-NiPS and (b) β-NiPS, respectively.

figure.head: Fig 3 (
figure.figDesc: a) andFig 3(b) show the calculated phonon dispersion of penta-NiPS for α and β phase, respectively. There are no imaginary modes throughout the Brillouin zone, confirming that both are dynamically stable. We note that the imperceptible negative frequencies at Γ, (visible only when zoomed

figure.head: Figure 3 :
figure.figDesc: Figure 3: Calculated phonon band structure of (a) α-NiPS and (b) β-NiPS.

12 )/C 11 and E b = (C 2 22 -C 2 12 )/C 22 . In the α phase, E a = 207.71 Nm -1 and E b = 178.14 Nm -1 . These values are close to those of penta-PdPSe with identical structure, E a = 218 Nm -1 and E b = 152 Nm -1 , calculated from DFT calculation. 12 Therefore, the α-NiPS are as strong as penta-PdPSe. In β phase, the calculated values are E a = 184.27 Nm -1 and E b = 140.50 Nm -1 , indicating that it is softer

For β-NiPS, the calculated Poisson's ratio on the [100] direction, ν a = C 12 /C 11 is 0.135 which is slightly lower than that on the the [010] direction, ν b = C 12 /C 22 of 0.156. Interestingly, for α-NiPS, the calculated Poisson's ratios are nearly zero for both [100] and [010] directions (0 • and 90 • ), ν a = 0.003 and ν b = 0.004. The nearly zero Poisson's ratios was previously observed in ternary penta-BCN bus it is located near [110] direction (∼ 45 • ) with the value of 0.004. 10 Moreover, Hou et al. 27 reported that the ternary penta-BCP exhibited the largest positive Poisson's ratio of 1.30 along the [010] direction, which is now the highest among 2D materials. The near zero Poisson's ratios of the present β-NiPS indicate that β-NiPS has an ability to maintain its dimensions under compression/extension along the in-plane directions.

figure.head: Figure 4 :
figure.figDesc: Figure 4: Variation of (a) in-plane Young's modulus, and (b) Poisson's ratio for α-NiPS and β-NiPS. Note that solid and dash line represent α-NiPS and β-NiPS, respectively.

figure.head: Figure 5 :
figure.figDesc: Figure 5: (a) the variation in biaxial strain energy (E s ) and (b) its derivative for α-NiPS and β-NiPS. Blue and red color represent α-NiPS and β-NiPS, respectively.

a) and S1(b) in the supplementary information (SI), the band gaps of both polymorphs under the strain seem to linearly decrease from 2.35 to 0.94 eV (α) and 2.20 to 1.00 eV (β), respectively. In α-NiPS, the valence band maximum (VBM) shifted from Y -Γ path to Y -M path while that of the conduction band minimum (CBM) changed from M point to Γ point. On the other hand, the characteristics of VBM and CBM of β-NiPS have not been altered with strain as depicted in Fig.S2(a) and S2(b) in SI.

figure.head: Figure 6 :
figure.figDesc: Figure 6: Total energy fluctuation with time during the AIMD simulation at 800 K of (a) α-NiPS and (b) β-NiPS.

with an indirect band gap of 2.35 and 2.20 eV, for α phase and β phase. They are soft materials with 2D Young's modulus of E a = 207.71 Nm -1 and E b = 178.14 Nm -1 for the α phase and E a = 184.27 Nm -1 and E b = 140.50 Nm -1 for the β phase. Interestingly, the α penta-NiPS exhibited a close to zero Poisson's ratio of 0.003-0.004. These values are significantly smaller than those of the β phase, 0.135-0.156. The nearly zero Poisson's ratios allow β-penta-NiPS to maintain its dimensions under extension with potential applications in wearable electronics.

figure.head: Table 1 :
figure.figDesc: The calculated lattice constants a and b in Å, bond lengths d 1 -d 6 in Å, bond angles θ 1 -θ 11 in degree, thicknesses T 1 and T 2 in Å, the cohesive energy per atom in eV/atom, and band gap E P BE

figure.head: Table 2 :
figure.figDesc: The elastic constants C jk (Nm -1 ), Poisson's ratio on [100] (ν a ) and [010] (ν b ) directions and Young's modulus (Nm -1 ) on [100] (E a ) and [010] (E b ) directions. phase.Table 2 lists the calculated Poisson's ratio.

div.head: Acknowledgement
div.p: This project is funded by National Research Council of Thailand (NRCT) and Kasetsart University : N42A650278. K.D. was supported by the National Research Council of Thailand (NRCT), No. NRCT5-RGJ63002-028. T.T. was supported by Graduate Program Scholarship from the Graduate School, Kasetsart University. S.J. has received funding support from the NSRF via the Program Management Unit for Human Resources & Institutional Development, Research and Innovation [grant number B05F640051]. We wish to thank NSTDA Supercomputer Center (ThaiSC) for providing computing resources for this work.

org.orgName: Program Management Unit for Human Resources & Institutional Development, Research and Innovation

div.p: The coordination of the optimized geometries of α and β penta-NiPS and additional figures as mentioned in the text.
